projects.title: LAutos — Vehicle Shipping Platform
projects.description: The application enables customers to browse available vehicles, place orders, upload payment proof, and track their shipment — with payments handled offline via bank transfer or Mobile Money. A full import duty and cost calculator will be delivered in Phase 2 as a standalone value-add tool to drive traffic and convert visitors into buyers.
projects.notes_from_cv: CV describes this project in more detail than the repo's About text: Laravel, Blade, Livewire, Alpine.js, Filament, Spatie Permissions, MySQL. Replaced a manual order process with a fully automated shipping platform featuring Google OAuth login, role-based access control, real-time tracking, Brevo email notifications, GiantSMS alerts, Sentry error monitoring, and automated deployments.
data_quality_flags: No README.md in the repo — only the GitHub 'About' description is available, so the tech-stack detail (Livewire, Filament, Spatie Permissions, Google OAuth, Brevo, GiantSMS, Sentry) comes from the CV, not the repo itself. Verify these are still accurate before publishing as case-study copy.

This is the same project flagged in the earlier /projects page scrape as mismatched under the 'koinonia-initiative' slug on the live WordPress site — use this GitHub data as the authoritative source instead.

projects.title: SubTrack — Client Subscription & Asset Manager
projects.description: SubTrack is a private internal tool for web agencies to track client subscriptions (domains, hosting, SSL, maintenance), automate renewal reminders, and generate professional USD invoices.
features: Dashboard: high-level overview of critical and warning expiries
Client & Project Management: organize assets by client
Subscription Tracking: monitor domains, hosting, and SSL with traffic-light status
Renewal Tracker: track provider vs. client costs and payment status
Invoicing: generate professional PDF invoices and send via email
Settings: configure agency details, logos, and invoice numbering
Automated daily expiry checks and email notifications via a scheduled artisan command (subtrack:check-expiries)
projects.notes_from_cv: Matches the CV's 'Subtrack — Subscription Tracker for Web Agencies' project: Laravel, Blade, Livewire, Alpine.js, DomPDF, MySQL. CV specifically credits it with eliminating missed renewal deadlines via Laravel scheduled jobs and generating professional USD-denominated PDF invoices — consistent with the README.
data_quality_flags: None significant — this repo has a clean, complete, and consistent README. Good candidate to lead with as a case study.

projects.title: OpenStores — The Operating System for Modern Retail
projects.description: OpenStores is a comprehensive, multi-tenant SaaS solution designed to streamline retail operations. From a lightning-fast Point of Sale (POS) to advanced inventory management and real-time analytics, OpenStores empowers independent retailers to compete with big-box giants.
features: Modern Point of Sale (POS): high-speed checkout, offline capable, device-agnostic (iPad/Android/Desktop), smart split/partial payments, USB and Bluetooth barcode scanner support
Advanced Inventory Management: real-time tracking across registers, low-stock alerts, product variants (size/color/material), real-time stock valuation
Analytics & Reporting: live dashboard for sales/profit/top products, daily/weekly/monthly reports, employee performance tracking
Multi-Business Architecture: SaaS-ready multi-tenancy, business switching for users managing multiple businesses, role-based access (Owners, Managers, Cashiers)
projects.notes_from_cv: Not explicitly named in the CV under 'Key Projects,' but the CV's Additional Projects line references 'OpenStore' among the list — this GitHub repo (openstorev2) appears to be a rebuilt/second version.
data_quality_flags: Repo name is 'openstorev2' but the product is branded 'OpenStores' (plural) in the README — decide which name to use consistently on the new site.

Only 4 commits despite a fairly comprehensive feature set described — worth confirming this reflects the actual current state of the app (e.g., is it functional/deployed anywhere, or mostly scaffolding/planning at this point) before writing case-study copy that implies a finished, in-production product.
No live URL found — the README links its own logo image to 'https://openstores.com' but this wasn't verified as a live, working site during this scrape.

projects.title: PodList — WordPress Podcast Playlist Plugin
projects.description: A modern WordPress plugin that makes it easy to embed podcast playlists from Spotify with customizable display options. Supports caching, pagination, load more/infinite scroll, and custom styling.
features: Embed Spotify podcast playlists via a simple shortcode: [podlist]
Load More button or Infinite Scroll pagination
Admin settings panel in the WordPress dashboard for Spotify Show URL, caching, limits, pagination, and appearance
projects.notes_from_cv: Matches the 'PodList' mention in the CV's Additional Projects line. This is a WordPress plugin, not a full-stack app — different category from the others, worth tagging separately (e.g. 'WordPress Plugin' vs 'Web Application') on the new site.
data_quality_flags: Clean, complete README — safe to seed largely as-is.
No screenshots included in the README — worth adding at least one showing the embedded playlist in action before publishing.

projects.title: Sikadaka — Financial Management Application
projects.description: A financial management application (per GitHub's 'About' field).
projects.notes_from_cv: Matches the /projects page case study already scraped: a Laravel-based financial management system for community savings groups, engineered to scale to 1,000+ members.
data_quality_flags: CRITICAL — the README.md in this repo is NOT about Sikadaka at all. It's a generic local-setup guide titled 'How To Setup Project on Locally' that repeatedly refers to a 'Funeral App' and instructs cloning 'git@github.com:oheneadj/funeralapp.git'. This looks like a README copy-pasted from an entirely different, unrelated project and never updated. Do not seed any of this README's content as Sikadaka's description — use the case-study copy already scraped from ohene.dev/projects (Challenge/Build/Impact) and the CV instead, and fix the README in the actual repo separately so anyone who visits it on GitHub isn't confused.

recommended_priority_for_new_case_studie: subtrack — cleanest data, clear features, matches CV exactly
podlist — clean data, but different category (WordPress plugin) so frame accordingly
lautos — good CV detail available despite no README, and it fixes the broken koinonia-initiative page
openstorev2 — verify current live/deployment status before writing case-study language that implies a finished product
